frameworks.name: model-based planning
frameworks.one_line: agents use internal transition models to plan sequential actions

frameworks.name: state abstraction
frameworks.one_line: filtering task-irrelevant features yields a shared low-dimensional transition model

frameworks.name: reinforcement learning (positive/negative outcome asymmetry)
frameworks.one_line: transition updates depend on outcome valence

core_question: How does the human medial temporal lobe represent state-transition models when input features may or may not matter for the task?
key_assumptions: planning requires an internal model of state transitions
irrelevant input features can be filtered to reduce dimensionality
BOLD multivariate patterns index the underlying transition representation
manipulations: task contexts in which visual input features were either relevant or irrelevant to transition structure
trials varying input features while holding state associations constant
key_findings: participants switched between low-dimensional (cue-independent) and high-dimensional (cue-specific) transition representations
medial temporal lobe BOLD patterns tracked learned state transitions
MTL pattern similarity across differing input features was lower in the high-dimensional than the low-dimensional case
transition models updated more after positive than negative outcomes
interpretations: MTL flexibly switches between shared and separable transition models according to task demands
model updating is asymmetric with respect to outcome valence
